SUMMARY:
Within this context, characterization techniques that probe the catalyst structure and the reaction as it is occurring (e.g. in-situ and _operando_) are exceptionally useful as they can elucidate catalyst transformations, reaction intermediates, and reaction environment to help piece together a more comprehensive picture of the reaction mechanism(s) at play3."). In this perspective, we define in-situ techniques as those being performed on a catalytic system as it is under simulated reaction conditions (e.g. elevated temperature, applied voltage, immersed in solvent, presence of reactants) while _operando_ techniques are those that probe the catalyst under the same, or as close as possible, conditions _and_ when its activity is being simultaneously measured. Probing the oxygen reduction reaction intermediates and dynamic active site structures of molecular and pyrolyzed Fe–N–C electrocatalysts by in-situ Raman spectroscopy.

FULL CONTENT:
Best practices for in-situ and operando techniques within electrocatalytic systems | Nature Communications

 Best practices for in-situ and _operando_ techniques within electrocatalytic systems

Best practices for in-situ and _operando_ techniques within electrocatalytic systems
====================================================================================

*   [Aditya Prajapati](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#auth-Aditya-Prajapati-Aff1)[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#Aff1), 
*   [Christopher Hahn](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#auth-Christopher-Hahn-Aff1)[ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0002-2772-6341](https://orcid.org/0000-0002-2772-6341)[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#Aff1),

*   [Yonghyuk Lee](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#auth-Yonghyuk-Lee-Aff4)[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#Aff4),

*   [Simon R. Bare](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#auth-Simon_R_-Bare-Aff5)[ORCID: orcid.org/0000-0002-4932-0342](https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4932-0342)[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#Aff5),

*   [Shuai Yan](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#auth-Shuai-Yan-Aff7)[7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#Aff7)&

Show authors
[_Nature Communications_](https://www.nature.com/ncomms)**volume 16**, Article number:2593 (2025) [Cite this article](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#citeas)

*   15k Accesses

*   5 Citations

*   10 Altmetric

*   [Metrics details](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6/metrics)

Abstract
--------

In-situ and _operando_ techniques in heterogeneous electrocatalysis are a powerful tool used to elucidate reaction mechanisms. Ultimately, they are key in determining concrete links between a catalyst’s physical/electronic structure and its activity en route to designing next-generation systems. To this end, the exact execution and interpretation of these lines of experiments is critical as this determines the strength of conclusions that can be drawn and what uncertainties remain. Instead of focusing on how techniques were used to understand systems, as is the case with most reviews on the topic, this work instead initiates a nuanced discussion of 1) how to best carry out each technique and 2) initiate a nuanced analysis of which level of insights can be drawn from the set of in-situ or _operando_ experiments/controls carried out. We focus on several commonly used techniques, including vibrational (IR, Raman) spectroscopy, X-ray absorption spectroscopy and electrochemical mass spectrometry. In addition to this, we include sections of reactor design and the link with theoretical modelling that are applicable across all techniques. While we focus on heterogeneous electrocatalysis, we make links when appropriate to the areas of photo- and thermo-catalytic systems. We highlight common pitfalls in the field, how to avoid them, and what sets of complementary experiments may be used to strengthen the analysis. We end with an overview of what gaps remain in in-situ and _operando_ techniques and what innovations must be made to overcome them.

Article 26 August 2024

Introduction
------------

Catalysts are instrumental in carrying out chemical transformations towards a desired product with high rates and selectivity. In the context of sustainability, the design of effective catalysts helps minimize the carbon footprint of the fuels and chemicals industries as their chemical production routes require less energy and minimize undesired by-products[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#ref-CR1 "Descorme, C., Gallezot, P., Geantet, C. & George, C. Heterogeneous catalysis: a key tool toward sustainability. ChemCatChem 4, 1897–1906 (2012)."). Catalyst innovation stands to also aid in the maturation of renewable-based electro and photochemical technologies to the point where they are technoeconomically competitive with conventional thermochemical systems[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#ref-CR2 "De Luna, P. et al. What would it take for renewably powered electrosynthesis to displace petrochemical processes? Science 364, eaav3506 (2019)."). These aforementioned thrusts are inherently linked to the United Nations (UN) Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) of affordable and clean energy (SDG 7), industry, innovation, and infrastructure (SDG 10), responsible consumption and production patterns (SDG 12) and climate action (SDG 13).

Underpinning the development of next-generation heterogeneous catalysts is, of course, a thorough mechanistic understanding of how they function, which precise reaction pathways are taking place on their surfaces, and the determination of the structure of this surface under reaction conditions. Within this context, characterization techniques that probe the catalyst structure and the reaction as it is occurring (e.g. in-situ and _operando_) are exceptionally useful as they can elucidate catalyst transformations, reaction intermediates, and reaction environment to help piece together a more comprehensive picture of the reaction mechanism(s) at play[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#ref-CR3 "Handoko, A. D., Wei, F., Jenndy, Yeo, B. S. & Seh, Z. W. Understanding heterogeneous electrocatalytic carbon dioxide reduction through operando techniques. Nat. Catal. 1, 922–934 (2018)."). In this perspective, we define in-situ techniques as those being performed on a catalytic system as it is under simulated reaction conditions (e.g. elevated temperature, applied voltage, immersed in solvent, presence of reactants) while _operando_ techniques are those that probe the catalyst under the same, or as close as possible, conditions _and_ when its activity is being simultaneously measured. These include considerations of mass transport, gas/liquid/solid interfaces, quantifying product formation, and more. Each in-situ/_operando_ technique provides different pieces of the puzzle. Alongside various performance measurements, ex-situ characterization and theoretical modelling, the information from in-situ/_operando_ experiments can be used to put together a plausible mechanism and to construct links between key characteristics of a catalyst and its macroscopically measured activity[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#ref-CR4 "Zhu, Y., Wang, J., Chu, H., Chu, Y.-C. & Chen, H. M. In-situ/operando studies for designing next-generation electrocatalysts. ACS Energy Lett. 5, 1281–1291 (2020)."),[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#ref-CR5 "Hasa, B., Zhao, Y. & Jiao, F. In-situ/operando characterization techniques of electrochemical CO2 reduction. Annu. Rev. Chem. Biomol. Eng. 14, 165–185 (2023)."),[6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#ref-CR6 "Favaro, M., Artiglia, L. & Mun, B. S. In-situ/operando investigation of catalytic and electrocatalytic interfaces. J. Phys. D: Appl. Phys. 55, 060201 (2022)."). To this end, some techniques like X-ray diffraction (XRD) are geared towards measuring the crystalline structure of a catalyst while others like X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) are more suited for measuring its local electronic and geometric structure under reaction conditions (Fig.[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#Fig1)). Complementary to this, techniques used to measure reactants, intermediates, and products include infrared (IR) and Raman spectroscopy (though the two can be useful for material analysis), as well as electrochemical mass spectrometry (ECMS). These primary techniques, while not being the only ones used, are the principal ones covered in this perspective. In addition, while this analysis will mainly focus on the techniques applied to heterogeneous electrocatalysis, we will also select key examples when the discussion is relevant to thermo- and photo-catalytic systems. Techniques of UV–Vis absorption, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, atomic force microscopy, transmission electron microscopy and others also provide important information but fall outside of the scope of this perspective.

**Fig. 1: Overview of analytical techniques for mechanistic studies of electrocatalytic systems.**

[![Image 5: figure 1](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-025-57563-6/MediaObjects/41467_2025_57563_Fig1_HTML.png)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6/figures/1)

Simplified illustration of the role of in-situ _, operando_ and analytical techniques, as well as individual information provided by them.

[Full size image](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6/figures/1)

As the use of in-situ and _operando_ techniques rapidly grows, there is correspondingly a growing number of reviews in the literature that discuss what the insights derived from the techniques were[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#ref-CR4 "Zhu, Y., Wang, J., Chu, H., Chu, Y.-C. & Chen, H. M. In-situ/operando studies for designing next-generation electrocatalysts. ACS Energy Lett. 5, 1281–1291 (2020)."). In contrast to this, there is not yet an adequate analysis dedicated to how to best carry out such experiments and what degree of conclusions (e.g. interpretation) can be drawn regarding the reaction mechanisms from the specific experiments carried out. Thus, the overall aim is to help the field’s development by proposing technique-dependent frameworks that can be followed to draw valid, translatable interpretations of in-situ and _operando_ data while minimizing often-encountered pitfalls such as false positives and mechanistic overreach. This perspective is structured differently than reviews that discuss how such techniques have been used for mechanism discovery and is rather concentrated on the process of carrying out the techniques and interpretation of resultant data. Further, as mechanisms are difficult to conclusively prove, we do not seek to have an absolute ‘yes or no’ checklist but will detail layers of supporting measurements that strengthen interpretation. We first discuss key criteria and limitations translatable across all in-situ and _operando_ measurements. These include reactor design and experimental cross-referencing of catalytic data. Next, we focus on three main classes of techniques used in heterogeneous electrocatalysts: XAS, vibrational (IR + Raman) spectroscopy, and ECMS. With each technique, we underline a base set of minimal experiments necessary and a progressive set of complementary experiments that can be used to further strengthen claims. This former includes the need to perform standard control experiments that lack the reactant/catalyst and properly correlating to previous literature examples, while the latter entails endeavors like isotope labeling and product measurement. We further detail how maximizing connections with multi-modal analysis, catalytic data and theoretical modelling can bolster findings, as well as how to avoid over-interpretation in this direction. Outlooks on big data and the role of machine learning in this direction are also discussed in a forward-looking conclusion. While this perspective focuses on heterogeneous electrocatalysis, we anticipate the general framework to be translatable to fields such as molecular catalysis to help accelerate the fields’ progression.

Reactor design
--------------

A crucial component of in-situ and _operando_ measurements is the use of a reactor that enables a researcher to incorporate or simulate reaction conditions while simultaneously using the characterization technique of choice. This often entails the implementation of optical windows to allow the portion of the electromagnetic spectrum to be incident on the catalyst that can be integrated into the analytical instrument as well as a modification of reactor dimensions. These alterations may consequently lead to differences in the catalyst environment between that in the “regular” reactor and in the in-situ/_operando_ cell. Against this backdrop, this section delves into the intricacies of reactor design for in-situ and _operando_ measurements in heterogeneous electrocatalysis, aiming to provide an overview of gaps and strategic recommendations for optimizing this critical aspect of catalysis research. While each technique has its inherent requirements for reactors, we use this section to identify generally translatable aspects in reactor design.

### Mismatch between the characterization and real-world experimental conditions

In-situ _/operando_ reactors are typically designed per the specifications required by the instruments for characterization. This introduces a significant difference in the transport of the species in benchmarking reactors vs. in-situ reactors. While electrolyte flow and gas diffusion electrodes are typically leveraged within benchmarking reactors to control convective and diffusive transport of species, most in-situ reactors are designed for batch operation and employ planar electrodes[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#ref-CR4 "Zhu, Y., Wang, J., Chu, H., Chu, Y.-C. & Chen, H. M. In-situ/operando studies for designing next-generation electrocatalysts. ACS Energy Lett. 5, 1281–1291 (2020)."),[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#ref-CR5 "Hasa, B., Zhao, Y. & Jiao, F. In-situ/operando characterization techniques of electrochemical CO2 reduction. Annu. Rev. Chem. Biomol. Eng. 14, 165–185 (2023)."),[6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#ref-CR6 "Favaro, M., Artiglia, L. & Mun, B. S. In-situ/operando investigation of catalytic and electrocatalytic interfaces. J. Phys. D: Appl. Phys. 55, 060201 (2022)."). Specific problems arising due to this change include poor mass transport of reactant species to the catalyst surface and a more drastic change in electrolyte composition for a batch system (e.g. pH gradients), leading to a change in microenvironment. This increases the likelihood of misinterpreting insights from such techniques. For example, Watkins et al. show that the reactor hydrodynamics controls Tafel slopes for CO 2 reduction (CO2R) by altering the microenvironment at the catalyst surface[7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#ref-CR7 "Watkins, N. B. et al. Hydrodynamics change Tafel slopes in electrochemical CO2 reduction on copper. ACS Energy Lett. 8, 2185–2192 (2023)."). Additionally, while an in-situ XAS batch reactor with planar oxide-derived Cu electrode showed that undercoordinated Cu sites promote binding of CO and enhance electrochemical activity[8](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#ref-CR8 "Verdaguer-Casadevall, A. et al. Probing the active surface sites for CO reduction on oxide-derived copper electrocatalysts. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 137, 9808–9811 (2015)."), another study in a vapour-fed device for CO reduction showed no correlation between Cu oxides and high electrochemical activity[9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#ref-CR9 "Lee, S. H. et al. Correlating oxidation state and surface area to activity from operando studies of copper CO electroreduction catalysts in a gas-fed device. ACS Catal. 10, 8000–8011 (2020)."). These examples emphasize the importance of species transport and analysis under benchmarking conditions as in-situ characterization work in batch reactors can likely have convoluted mass transport effects, limiting the ability to attribute mechanistic conclusions to intrinsic reaction kinetics.

Furthermore, sub-optimal _operando_ reactor designs can significantly impact the response time and signal-to-noise ratio of measurements, leading to increased data acquisition time and potentially obscuring short-timescale reaction events. The path through which a spectroscopic beam travels and the path length between a reaction event and the spectroscopic probe is pivotal for rapid and precise data collection. Poor design can result in delayed response time and increased residence time of species, decreasing the probability of observing short-lived reaction intermediates. For instance, in differential electrochemical mass spectrometry (DEMS), the reactor configuration controls the proximity of the pervaporation membrane to the electrode, tuning the response time of the mass spectrometer to reactive species. With regards to best practices in eliminating long response times, Clark and Bell addressed this by depositing a CO2R catalyst directly onto the pervaporation membrane in a DEMS electrochemical cell thereby eliminating a long path length between CO2R intermediates generating at the catalyst surface and the mass spectrometry probe[10](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#ref-CR10 "Clark, E. L. & Bell, A. T. Direct observation of the local reaction environment during the electrochemical reduction of CO2. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 140, 7012–7020 (2018)."). This enabled them to detect much larger concentrations of acetaldehyde and propionaldehyde (intermediates for ethanol and n-propanol, respectively) at the surface of the catalyst compared to their concentrations in the bulk.

Similarly, in grazing incidence X-ray diffraction (GIXRD), co-optimizing X-ray transmission through the liquid electrolyte and the beam’s interaction area at the catalyst surface is crucial for a good signal-to-noise ratio. Farmand et al. showed that in-situ GIXRD requires careful consideration of both the path and path length for the incident beam to minimize contact with the aqueous electrolyte, preventing signal attenuation while ensuring that the beam interacts with a sufficient surface area of the catalyst to generate useful signals rapidly[11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#ref-CR11 "Farmand, M. et al. Electrochemical flow cell enabling operando probing of electrocatalyst surfaces by X-ray spectroscopy and diffraction. Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys. 21, 5402–5408 (2019)."). Therefore, co-designing reactors with spectroscopic probes is necessary to bridge the gap between characterization and real-world experimental conditions.

Additionally, direct in-situ spectroscopic characterization within zero-gap reactors has been challenging as typical cell components can be opaque to measurement probes such as infrared, Raman, and X-rays. Many measurements for reactions such as CO2R, oxygen evolution reaction (OER), and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) fall short of matching the complexities of zero-gap configurations and current densities of high-performance operation, diminishing the industrial relevance of mechanistic conclusions. Literature from more mature electrocatalytic processes such as fuel cells and some recent reports on CO2R recommend modification of the end plates of zero-gap reactors with beam-transparent windows to enable _operando_ XAS to circumvent the challenges associated with opaque components[12](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#ref-CR12 "Meyer, Q., Zeng, Y. & Zhao, C. In-situ and operando characterization of proton exchange membrane fuel cells. Adv. Mater. 31, 1901900 (2019)."),[13](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#ref-CR13 "El-Nagar, G. A., Haun, F., Gupta, S., Stojkovikj, S. & Mayer, M. T. Unintended cation crossover influences CO2 reduction selectivity in Cu-based zero-gap electrolysers. Nat. Commun. 14, 2062 (2023)."). Hence, to bridge the gap of the reactor transport discrepancy, optimizing electrochemical reactors for _operando_ measurements requires simultaneous consideration of design criteria for benchmarking and in-situ characterization.

### Standardization challenges in reactor design for in-situ and _Operando_ techniques

Variability in reactor geometry and operation poses a challenge when comparing the results of in-situ _/operando_ experiments between different research groups. Each group may have collected the data in different operating regimes, where their system could be either kinetically limited, diffusion-limited, or in an intermediate regime. This often leads to different interpretations for product selectivity and reaction mechanisms and has been a frequent occurrence in the more explored electrocatalytic reactions such as CO2R[14](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#ref-CR14 "Lin, S.-C. et al. Operando time-resolved X-ray absorption spectroscopy reveals the chemical nature enabling highly selective CO2 reduction. Nat. Commun. 11, 3525 (2020)."),[15](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#ref-CR15 "Long, C. et al. Regulating reconstruction of oxide-derived Cu for electrochemical CO2 reduction toward n-propanol. Sci. Adv. 9, eadi6119 (2023)."). Different research groups often design reactors with varying electrode configurations, flow cell designs, and electrolyte management systems. This results in different local pH levels, CO 2 concentration gradients, and product distribution even under macroscopically similar experimental conditions[16](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#ref-CR16 "Mendoza, D., Dong, S.-T. & Lassalle-Kaiser, B. In-situ/operando X-ray spectroscopy applied to electrocatalytic CO2 reduction: status and perspectives. Curr. Opin. Colloid Interface Sci. 61, 101635 (2022)."),[17](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#ref-CR17 "Mom, R. V. et al. Assessment of the degradation mechanisms of Cu electrodes during the CO2 reduction reaction. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 15, 30052–30059 (2023)."). These discrepancies often stem from differences in mass transport and local reaction environments, influenced by the specific reactor designs. This highlights the broader challenge within the community of standardizing reactor designs to ensure that the mechanistic insights derived from in-situ experiments are directly comparable across different studies. Alternatively, another source of variability can arise from separately conducting benchmarking measurements and in-situ experiments and then correlating the results from the two by claiming it as _operando_ measurements. This is a common practice in CO2R and nitrogen reduction studies where online product analysis is possible[18](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#ref-CR18 "Yang, F. et al. Operando analysis reveals potential-driven in-situ formation of single-Fe-atom electrocatalysts for green production of ammonia. Proc. Natl Acad. Sci. USA 120, e2301011120 (2023)."),[19](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-57563-6#ref-CR19 "Timoshenko, J. & Roldan Cuenya, B. In-situ/operando electrocatalyst characterization by X-ray absorption spectroscopy. Chem. Rev. 121, 882–961 (2021)."). Rese

Review

_In situ_ characterization techniques and methodologies for high-temperature electrochemistry
=====================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Rui Yuan 1, Handong Jiao 1, Hongmin Zhu 3, [Daining Fang 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/author/7202133612/daining-fang), Shuqiang Jiao 2

The bigger picture
------------------

With carbon footprint reduction and the looming crisis of fossil fuel availability, high-temperature electrochemistry (HTE) driven by green energy is being intensively pursued. However, due to the harsh reaction conditions of HTE (high temperature, corrosion, thermal radiation, sealing, etc.) and _ex situ_-based research, macroscale characterization of HTE is relatively imprecise. The desire for in-depth research has driven increasing efforts toward _in situ_ characterization techniques and analytical methods in HTE. In this review, we have comprehensively sorted out and evaluated the _in situ_ techniques in the HTE field and pointed out an appropriate optimization direction. At the same time, we provide a methodology for HTE research, including experiments, theories, and simulations, and look forward to the realization of HTE prediction, optimization, and other functions by advanced artificial intelligence techniques (machine learning, digital twins, etc.).

Summary
-------

High-temperature [electrochemistry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electrochemistry "Learn more about electrochemistry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (HTE) plays an important role in basic industries, including [metallurgy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/metallurgy "Learn more about metallurgy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and energy and future frontier technologies such as [deep space exploration](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/deep-space-exploration "Learn more about deep space exploration from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and carbon neutralization. At present, traditional research methods based on _ex situ_, [macroscale](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/macroscale "Learn more about macroscale from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") characterization, and theory analogy with room-temperature [electrochemistry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/electrochemistry "Learn more about electrochemistry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") cannot meet the demands of researchers. _In situ_ characterization techniques can monitor real-time information, which can establish a full spatiotemporal, multidimensional, cross-scale methodology with the support of theoretical calculations, numerical simulations, and so on. It is hence of urgency to summarize and outlook the _in situ_ characterization techniques and analytical methods of HTE. This review focuses on the _in situ_ characterization techniques in HTE and points to a future direction in order to construct a methodology for HTE research. We therefore call on more researchers to enter the field of HTE in order to drive HTE engineering to realize low-carbon, high efficiency, refinement, and intelligence.

Introduction
------------

Since the voltaic [battery](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/battery-electrochemical-energy-engineering "Learn more about battery from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") was invented in 1800, [electrochemistry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electrochemistry "Learn more about electrochemistry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") has been inseparable from human society, and according to the working temperature, high-temperature [electrochemistry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/electrochemistry "Learn more about electrochemistry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (HTE) has been defined as an important branch. However, it is not convenient to divide electrochemistry into [room temperature](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/ambient-reaction-temperature "Learn more about room temperature from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and high temperature with specific numbers. Considering that aqueous electrochemistry and many characterization techniques are difficult to carry out above the [boiling point](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/boiling-point "Learn more about boiling point from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of water, an electrochemical system with electrolyte temperature above 100°C could be regarded as HTE. The electrolyte systems used in HTE can be divided into three categories from low to [high melting points](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/high-melting-point "Learn more about high melting points from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), namely, [ionic liquids](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/ionic-liquid "Learn more about ionic liquids from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), inorganic [molten salts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/molten-salt "Learn more about molten salts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and molten [oxides](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/oxide-compound "Learn more about oxides from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib1),[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib2),[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib3),[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib4) Although most [ionic liquids](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/ionic-liquid "Learn more about ionic liquids from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") still operate below 100°C or even at room temperature,[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib3) then HTE generally refers to the research carried out in molten salt and [oxide systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/oxide-system "Learn more about oxide systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). As the extension of traditional room-temperature electrochemistry, electrolytes used in HTE are promising media to break the limits of aqueous solutions due to their advantages such as high conductivity, wide electrochemical window, fast mass transfer, high solubility for reactants, and low [vapor pressure](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/vapor-pressure "Learn more about vapor pressure from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib5) Moreover, HTE has temperature advantages, which can not only trigger the reaction that cannot be carried out at room temperature from the view of thermodynamics but also accelerate the reaction rate to several orders of magnitude greater than that at room temperature from the view of kinetics. As early as the 19th century, HTE has achieved significant milestones, especially Davy’s separation of [alkali metals](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/alkali-metal "Learn more about alkali metals from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") from alkali [metal chloride](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/metal-chloride "Learn more about metal chloride from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") melts, Faraday’s establishment of the laws of electrolysis using lead [halide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/halide "Learn more about halide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") melts, and the electrolytic preparation of [aluminum](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/aluminum "Learn more about aluminum from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") invented by Hall and Heroult.[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib6)

To date, HTE has become an important basis for many disciplines, such as [metallurgy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/metallurgy "Learn more about metallurgy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (extraction, purification, and electroplating of most metals), chemical industry (carbon dioxide capture and conversion, [graphitization](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/graphitization "Learn more about graphitization from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of biomass and [carbon materials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/carbon-material "Learn more about carbon materials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and electrothermal catalysis), energy (liquid metal [battery](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/battery-electrochemical-energy-engineering "Learn more about battery from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and high-temperature sodium-sulfur battery), materials (synthesis of two-dimensional materials, carbon materials, and ceramic materials), nuclear industry (nuclear fuel refining and [spent fuel](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/spent-fuels "Learn more about spent fuel from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") reprocessing), corrosion, and protection (molten salt heat storage and heat transfer)[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib7),[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib8),[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib9),[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib10),[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib11),[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib12),[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib13),[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib14),[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib15),[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib16) ([Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#fig1)).

Figure 1. Applications of high-temperature electrochemistry

More importantly, with the reduction of carbon footprint, HTE driven by green energy will play a key role undoubtedly, including green power efficient utilization (high-temperature [electrochemical energy storage](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/electrochemical-energy-storage "Learn more about electrochemical energy storage from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") device[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib17)), CO 2 conversion and utilization (capture and electrochemical conversion of CO 2[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib18)), and the future [decarbonization](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/decarbonization "Learn more about decarbonization from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of traditional manufacturing based on green power (such as ironmaking by molten oxide electrolysis to replace traditional [blast furnace](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/blast-furnace "Learn more about blast furnace from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib19)). On the other hand, HTE also has great potential in [deep space exploration](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/deep-space-exploration "Learn more about deep space exploration from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). For example, the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) has successfully used [carbon dioxide](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/carbon-dioxide "Learn more about carbon dioxide from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the [Martian atmosphere](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/martian-atmosphere "Learn more about Martian atmosphere from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to produce oxygen by high-temperature electrolysis.[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib20) In addition, the China National Space Administration (CNSA) plans to use the molten oxide electrolysis method to process [lunar soil](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/lunar-soil "Learn more about lunar soil from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for preparing oxygen. Besides, the European Space Agency hopes to send the FFC-Cambridge process to the moon in 2025 for _in situ_ oxygen production.

However, in the past traditional sectors, research on HTE is relatively imprecise, which is commonly based on the _ex situ_, [macroscale](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/macroscale "Learn more about macroscale from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") experimental characterization and analogy with the theoretical analysis of room-temperature electrochemistry. Therefore, traditional research methods are limited by information accuracy and analysis ability, which can no longer meet the demands of research workers. In the present literature, _in situ_ characterization techniques and analytical methods of HTE are scarce. This situation may be attributed to the harsh reaction conditions of HTE (high temperature, corrosion, [thermal radiation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/thermal-radiation "Learn more about thermal radiation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), sealing, multifield coupling, etc.) and the complex operation process, which result in the difficulty of applying _in situ_ characterization techniques to HTE directly. On the other hand, as mentioned above, HTE was often used in traditional industrial fields in the past; hence, it did not receive the attention of frontier researchers. Affected by these factors, the cognition of the actual physical and chemical processes inside HTE is insufficient, which severely limits the development of HTE. For instance, due to the lack of evolutionary details of [anodic dissolution](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/anodic-dissolution "Learn more about anodic dissolution from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") behavior, it is difficult to build a mathematical model to rationally design and optimize the process.[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib21) Under the global background of commitment to energy structure conversion and high efficiency, green industrialization, with the rapid development of frontier science and technology and the deep integration of traditional industry, the slow pace of HTE has been difficult to meet the low-carbon, high efficiency, refinement, intelligent, customized goals in the international strategy of Industry 4.0 (5.0) and the macro-progress of space business. In order to make up the increasing gap between HTE discipline and modern manufacturing, it is urgent to establish a new methodology that combines experiments and theory to exploit the potential. At the same time, as a discipline closely related to industrial production, the basic research of HTE needs to be driven by experiments and integrate theoretical innovations to develop new research strategies and process control methods suitable for HTE.

Although several reviews of _in situ_ electrochemical characterization in specific fields have been reported,[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib22),[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib23),[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib24),[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib25),[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib26) current research is still far from supporting HTE research methodology.[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib27),[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib28),[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib29),[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib30) Thus, more researchers, especially those from different disciplines, are urgently needed to introduce more interdisciplinary technologies, theories, and equipment to promote the development of HTE. Furthermore, it is crucial to summarize the existing _in situ_ characterization techniques and analysis methods in HTE to attract interest.

In this review, we have comprehensively sorted out and evaluated the current _in situ_ electrochemistry techniques in HTE research, and various techniques for HTE are classified from the perspective of electrode-electrolyte structure ([Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#fig2)). The experimental techniques, theoretical calculation, and numerical simulation for establishing the full spatiotemporal, cross-scale characterization techniques, and analysis method for HTE are emphatically discussed. Based on the lack of spatiotemporal scale at present, we point out the appropriate optimization direction for different technologies in the future. At the same time, we look forward to the realization of HTE simulation, prediction, optimization, and other functions by advanced artificial intelligence (AI) technologies (machine learning [ML], digital twins, etc.), so as to construct the blocks of methodology for HTE research. Finally, we hope that this has attracted extensive interest in HTE research. We call on more researchers to enter the field of HTE in order to drive HTE engineering to realize low-carbon, high efficiency, refinement, and intelligence.

Electroanalytical methods
-------------------------

[Electroanalytical methods](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/electroanalytical-method "Learn more about Electroanalytical methods from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") can perform qualitative and quantitative analyses by measuring [electrochemical properties](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/electrochemical-property "Learn more about electrochemical properties from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which have the characteristics of high sensitivity, convenient operation, fast analysis speed, and wide application. However, the conclusion of electroanalytical methods is often based on speculation, and the analytical ability is limited.

The electroanalytical methods are mature after a long period of development. Many articles and books have been published to provide detailed explanations of it. Therefore, this review selects the most commonly used electroanalytical methods for introduction, which does not mean that only these methods have been used in [HTE](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electrochemistry "Learn more about HTE from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

### Potential sweep methods

The potential sweep method directly records the current-potential curve. The common potential sweep methods include cyclic [voltammetry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/voltammetry "Learn more about voltammetry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (CV), square wave [voltammetry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/voltammetry "Learn more about voltammetry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (SWV), and other methods.

In the past 100 years, CV has received widespread attention ([Figure 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#fig3)A).[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib31) This method could obtain qualitative information on [electrochemical reactions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electrochemical-reaction "Learn more about electrochemical reactions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") through current-voltage curves, also known as voltammogram. From the peak current and potential, we can deduce information regarding thermodynamics and kinetics of [electrode reactions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electrode-reaction "Learn more about electrode reactions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), [electron transfer](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electron-transport "Learn more about electron transfer from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") mechanisms, coupling chemical reactions, etc. A typical CV curve ([Figure 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#fig3)B) includes positive and negative scans, reflecting the [redox reactions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/redox-process "Learn more about redox reactions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and other [physicochemical processes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/physicochemical-process "Learn more about physicochemical processes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") involving redox species (adsorption, homogeneous chemical reactions, and disproportionation).[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2451929423003236#bib32)

SUMMARY:
In situ characterization refers to the analysis of a system or process while it is occurring, without disrupting or altering the conditions.

enriched_points.expanded_content: In-situ methods provide real-time insights into catalytic processes, revealing dynamic changes in structure and composition under operational conditions, enhancing understanding of reaction mechanisms.
Ex-situ techniques often fail to capture transient states and intermediates, leading to potential misinterpretations and incomplete mechanistic insights due to altered conditions during analysis.
In-situ spectroscopic techniques are pivotal in fields like electrocatalysis and materials science, providing critical data for optimizing catalysts and understanding their performance in real-time.

Future advancements include integrating machine learning with in-situ techniques to enhance data interpretation, improve sensitivity, and facilitate the development of next-generation catalytic systems.
